In the vast expanse of Mars, Utopia Planitia emerged as the prime candidate for the landing site, a rugged plain where the U.S. lander Viking 2 had made its historic descent in 1976. The China National Space Administration (CNSA) outlined ambitious goals for their mission with Tianwen-1, seeking to analyze and map the Martian surface and its geology, search for elusive water ice, and study the planet's climate and environmental conditions. At the heart of this mission was a solar-powered rover, weighing approximately 530 pounds (240 kilograms). If it touched down successfully, the rover would embark on an exploration of the Martian surface, utilizing its Subsurface Exploration Radar to examine soil characteristics and the distribution of potential water-ice. It was equipped with panoramic and multispectral cameras, designed to analyze the intricate composition of rocks scattered across the terrain.

The Tianwen-1 mission, along with the opportunity for the public to name the rover, had captivated the attention of many. On July 23, 2020, a Mars probe embarked on its journey, launched into the vastness of space atop a Long March-5 rocket from the Wenchang Spacecraft Launch Site nestled in the southern part of Hainan Province, China. As the probe soared away from Earth, the most daunting challenge loomed ahead: a delicate soft landing anticipated for May. This intricate maneuver, entirely autonomous, would unfold over a span of seven to eight minutes, a critical moment as the probe employed its aerodynamic design alongside a parachute and retrorocket to slow its descent. Legs would extend to cushion the final touch down, as detailed by the China National Space Administration (CNSA).

Chinese engineers and scientists had meticulously selected a relatively flat expanse in the southern reaches of Utopia Planitia, a vast plain on Mars, as the proposed landing zone. Insights from earlier studies suggested that this site might lie near the remnants of an ancient ocean or lake, hinting at a wetter past for the Martian landscape. With a keen sense of anticipation, Chinese scientists prepared to uncover more evidence of water-ice, a key to understanding Mars' history.

The mission, known as Tianwen-1, was driven by ambitious scientific goals. It aimed to map the intricate morphology and geological structure of the Martian surface, investigate the characteristics of the soil, and assess the distribution of water-ice. Furthermore, the probe would analyze the composition of surface materials, measure the ionosphere, and delve into the Martian climate and environmental conditions. It would also seek to uncover the physical fields and internal structure of the red planet itself.

Equipped with an array of seven scientific instruments, the orbiter was ready for its quest: two remote-sensing cameras, the Mars-Orbiting Subsurface Exploration Radar, the Mars Mineralogy Spectrometer, the Mars Magnetometer, and both the Mars Ion and Neutral Particle Analyzer and the Mars Energetic Particle Analyzer. Each tool was poised to unveil the mysteries that lay hidden beneath the Martian surface, as the probe journeyed deeper into the unknown.

As the clock ticked down to the final moments of December 31, 2021, the Tianwen-1 orbiter accomplished a remarkable feat, deploying a second deployable camera, known as TDC-2, into the vast expanse of Mars orbit. This camera captured stunning photographs of the orbiter itself, a celebration of its achievements over the past year. Just a few weeks later, on January 30, 2022, the orbiter's selfie stick payload was unfurled into action, snapping images of its own components alongside the proud display of the Chinese flag, a tribute to the upcoming Chinese New Year. 

The journey didn’t end there; by September 2022, the mission was honored with the prestigious World Space Award by the International Astronautical Federation, marking a significant milestone in its history. Tianwen-1 was the second of three Martian exploration missions launched during the July 2020 window, following the United Arab Emirates Space Agency's Hope orbiter and preceding NASA's Mars 2020 mission, which would eventually land the Perseverance rover and its companion, the Ingenuity helicopter drone.

China's foray into planetary exploration is officially recognized as the "Tianwen Series," with "Tianwen-1" (天问一号) standing as the inaugural mission. Future planetary endeavors will follow in numerical sequence, each one building on the legacy of this groundbreaking initiative. The name "Tianwen," meaning "questions to heaven" or "quest for heavenly truth," echoes the sentiments of an ancient poem penned by Qu Yuan, a revered Chinese poet from around 340–278 BC, reflecting the timeless human pursuit of knowledge and discovery.

In a pivotal video call with the banks that had supported his acquisition, Elon Musk reassured them of his commitment to finalize the deal by the looming deadline. On October 26, he made a bold visit to Twitter's headquarters, capturing the moment in a tweet where he playfully carried a kitchen sink through the lobby, even updating his Twitter bio to read "Chief Twit." During this visit, he addressed the Twitter employees, acknowledging that while layoffs were still a possibility, they would not occur on the extensive scale previously reported by the media.

The following day, Musk penned an open letter to advertisers, emphasizing that Twitter would not devolve into a "free-for-all hellscape." He clarified that his motivations for the acquisition stemmed not from greed but from an earnest desire to cultivate "a common digital town square." Seeking to bridge the gap between Tesla and Twitter, he called upon his engineers to collaborate with Twitter's product managers to evaluate the platform's codebase, which had been frozen until November 1.

Then, on the afternoon of October 27, the deal was sealed, and Musk triumphantly tweeted, "the bird is freed." With that declaration, he officially took the reins of Twitter, swiftly dismissing CEO Parag Agrawal, CFO Ned Segal, legal executive Vijaya Gadde, and general counsel Sean Edgett, who were escorted out of the headquarters by security, marking a dramatic shift in the company's leadership.

A week passed, and the atmosphere was charged with uncertainty as it emerged that Musk was under investigation by the U.S. government regarding his conduct in the proposed buyout. He confidently expressed his belief that Twitter's long-term value would surpass the price of $54.20 per share, deeming it an overpayment. Meanwhile, on October 20, The Washington Post unveiled Musk's intentions to slash Twitter's workforce by 75 percent. This revelation sent ripples through the company, as Twitter executives were eager to sell to Musk in hopes of alleviating their planned cuts to payroll and infrastructure. In response, Twitter employees rallied together, penning an open letter that condemned Musk's plans and warned of dire consequences for the platform's future. Adding to the tension, Bloomberg News and The Post reported that officials in the Biden administration were contemplating a national security review of Musk's acquisition and his other ventures through the Committee on Foreign Investment (CFIUS), hinting at the possibility of President Joe Biden intervening to block the purchase if necessary. The White House, however, was quick to dismiss these reports. As the clock ticked toward October 21, the bankers and lawyers representing both parties were poised to finalize the paperwork for the acquisition by the month's end, with hopes that the deal would close as anticipated. The banks facilitating the acquisition were prepared to absorb the $13 billion in debt rather than offloading it.

In the bustling heart of San Francisco, Twitter, a company boasting a workforce of over 7,500, found itself grappling with the challenge of consistently expanding its advertising-driven business and luring in new users. On a pivotal Thursday, the abrupt firings of top executives sent ripples through the corporate landscape, signaling that Mr. Musk was poised to act decisively. At 51, he was ready to reshape Twitter, now free from the obligation to unveil its performance every few months. By choosing to take the company private, he could sidestep the scrutiny of shareholders, allowing him to implement changes away from the watchful gaze of the public.

Mr. Musk, who simultaneously helmed the electric car giant Tesla and the aerospace company SpaceX, had yet to respond to requests for comment. The completion of his acquisition was celebrated as a triumph by Twitter's board. When he first agreed to pay $54.20 per share in April, the company faced backlash for what many perceived as an undervaluation. However, as the global economy stumbled and Twitter's stock took a hit in the months that followed, that initial price began to seem like a stroke of luck for shareholders. Determined to hold Mr. Musk to his word, the board pushed for him to honor the agreement.

As the dust settled on that consequential Thursday, the executives who lost their positions included Parag Agrawal, the chief executive; Ned Segal, the chief financial officer; Vijaya Gadde, the lead legal and policy executive; and Sean Edgett, the general counsel, according to two sources familiar with the situation.

On a crisp April day in 2022, the National Urban League took a stand, urging Twitter to reject Elon Musk's takeover bid. They voiced their concerns, warning of the potential threats to users' civil rights that such a move might entail. Meanwhile, a chorus of conservative commentators and politicians in the U.S. cheered on Musk, convinced that his proposed changes would rectify what they perceived as Twitter's bias against right-wing voices. As the days unfolded, U.S. House Republicans made their demands clear, insisting that Twitter's board retain all records related to Musk’s proposal, hinting at the possibility of a congressional investigation after the upcoming midterms.

In Florida, Jimmy Patronis, the state's Chief Financial Officer, expressed his approval of Musk's offer, criticizing Twitter's "poison pill" strategy as counterproductive. Amid the swirling opinions, a poll from Harvard University's Center for American Political Studies and the Harris Poll revealed that 57 percent of American voters were in favor of Musk's acquisition of Twitter. 

Amidst the uncertainty, Twitter's CEO, Parag Agrawal, took a moment to reassure his employees, commending their hard work despite the surrounding turbulence. He held an all-hands meeting on April 29 to directly address their worries, promising that layoffs were not on the horizon. Jack Dorsey, the former CEO, threw his support behind the sale, declaring that reclaiming Twitter from Wall Street was a crucial first step and expressing his trust in Musk as the new owner. Yet, not everyone was in agreement; Dick Costolo, another former CEO of Twitter, criticized Musk's harsh words about the platform, adding yet another layer to the unfolding drama.

In the unfolding story of Twitter, a network that captures the pulse of current events and conversations, a team of financial and legal experts has gathered to navigate the complexities of a significant acquisition. Advisors from Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC, J.P. Morgan, and Allen & Co. have stepped forward to offer their financial wisdom, while the legal intricacies are being handled by Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati, Professional Corporation, along with Simpson Thacher & Bartlett LLP. In this high-stakes scenario, Morgan Stanley takes the lead as the primary financial advisor to Elon Musk, with BofA Securities and Barclays also lending their expertise.

As the narrative unfolds, Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom LLP emerges as the legal counsel, ensuring that all proceedings are meticulously managed. The tale of Twitter, Inc. (NYSE: TWTR) is one of evolution and engagement, where the platform serves as a stage for the latest happenings and discussions. For those eager to delve deeper, a visit to about.twitter.com and a follow on @Twitter opens the door to more insights.

In the backdrop of this acquisition, Twitter, along with its directors and certain executive officers, is actively engaging in the solicitation of proxies from shareholders, a crucial step in the unfolding transaction. As part of this process, Twitter plans to submit a proxy statement, aptly named the "Transaction Proxy Statement," to the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), detailing the solicitation efforts to gain approval for the acquisition. This document will reveal additional information about the participants involved, shedding light on their interests and security holdings, as the narrative of this pivotal moment continues to develop.

query: How much did Elon Musk bought Twitter?
prediction: According to the document, Elon Musk initially proposed to buy Twitter for $31 billion and later $39.6 billion, but ultimately agreed to buy the platform for $44 billion.
docs:
On a brisk day in April 2022, business magnate Elon Musk embarked on an ambitious journey to acquire the American social media giant, Twitter, Inc. His interest had been kindled months earlier when he began purchasing shares in January, steadily amassing a significant stake that made him the largest shareholder by April, with ownership reaching 9.1 percent. Twitter, recognizing Musk's growing influence, extended an invitation for him to join its board of directors. Though he initially accepted the offer, Musk soon chose to decline, setting the stage for a dramatic turn of events.

On April 14, Musk boldly made an unsolicited bid to buy Twitter, a move that startled the company's board. In a bid to fend off what they perceived as a hostile takeover, they enacted a “poison pill” strategy. However, by April 25, the tide had turned; the board unanimously agreed to Musk's buyout offer of a staggering $44 billion. Musk had grand plans for the platform—promising new features, an open-source approach to its algorithms, a crackdown on spambot accounts, and a commitment to promoting free speech.

But as summer settled in, the narrative took another twist. In July, Musk declared his intention to back out of the deal, claiming that Twitter had breached their agreement by not taking sufficient action against spambot accounts. This prompted Twitter to retaliate, filing a lawsuit against Musk in the Delaware Court of Chancery, with a trial looming on the horizon for the week of October 17.

Yet, just weeks before the trial was to commence, Musk surprised many by reversing his decision, announcing that he would indeed proceed with the acquisition. The culmination of this high-stakes saga arrived on October 27, when the deal was finalized. With a decisive stroke, Musk became Twitter's new owner and CEO, transforming the company into a private entity under a new parent company called X Corp.

In the unfolding drama of corporate maneuvers, Elon Musk found himself in a complex dance with Twitter. He had initially proposed to buy the platform for $31 billion and later $39.6 billion, but both offers were met with rejection by the company. However, on October 3, a significant twist occurred. Musk's legal team reached out to Twitter, revealing that Musk had experienced a change of heart. He was ready to proceed with his original offer of $54.20 per share, but only if Twitter agreed to drop its lawsuit against him. This shift was driven by concerns from Musk's team, who feared they might struggle to demonstrate a material adverse effect that would justify breaking the contract.

As the clock ticked down, depositions for Musk and Twitter CEO Parag Agrawal were set for October 6 and 10, respectively. Musk's vision for acquiring Twitter was ambitious; he envisioned it as a cornerstone of an "everything app" known as X, a platform that would encompass a variety of services. In the midst of this unfolding narrative, Judge Kathaleen McCormick sought clarity from both parties on how to proceed. 

Musk's announcement sparked excitement, sending Twitter shares soaring by 23 percent. Yet, as the dust settled, neither Musk nor Twitter responded to McCormick's inquiry, leading her to declare that the trial would continue as scheduled. On October 6, however, Musk successfully requested a postponement of the trial to October 28, allowing him to finalize his debt financing for the acquisition. McCormick noted that if the deal did not close by then, the trial would be rescheduled for November, leaving everyone on the edge of their seats, awaiting the next chapter in this high-stakes saga.

In a bold financial maneuver, Musk orchestrated a complex funding arrangement to support his acquisition of Twitter. This intricate web comprised $7 billion in senior secured bank loans, alongside $6 billion in subordinated debt. Notably, there was also $6.25 billion in bank loans secured personally by Musk, backed by an impressive $62.5 billion of his Tesla stock. Additionally, Musk pledged $20 billion in cash equity, sourced from the sale of his Tesla shares and other assets, while 19 independent investors contributed $7.1 billion in equity.

As the negotiations unfolded, the initial proposal of borrowing $13 billion for Twitter raised eyebrows; it represented a staggering seven times the company's projected operating cash flow for 2022. Some banks deemed this multiple too risky, choosing instead to participate only in a more modest $12.5 billion margin loan to Musk. The implications of this debt were significant, with estimates suggesting that Twitter would face around $1 billion in annual interest and fees.

Just two days following the announcement of his bid, Musk took strategic steps by registering three holding companies under the name "X Holdings," laying the groundwork for his takeover. However, the market reacted swiftly; Tesla shares plummeted by 12 percent the day after the acquisition news broke, reflecting smaller declines across broader markets. This shift resulted in Musk facing a staggering $21 billion paper loss. Within a mere three days after Twitter consented to the acquisition, Musk liquidated $8.5 billion worth of his Tesla shares, signaling a rapid response to the unfolding financial landscape.

On May 4, the Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee of the House of Commons in the United Kingdom called upon Elon Musk to discuss the implications of his buyout on free speech and the issue of "online harms." The very next day, Musk secured an impressive $7.1 billion in additional funding, garnering support from notable figures and firms, including Oracle Corporation co-founder Larry Ellison, Saudi prince Al Waleed bin Talal Al Saud, and the venture capital giants Andreessen Horowitz and Sequoia Capital, as well as the sovereign wealth fund Qatar Holding. This new influx of equity dramatically reduced Musk's initial $12.5 billion personal bank loan to $6.25 billion and lowered his cash equity requirement from $21 billion to just under $20 billion.

As the days unfolded, on May 11, The Wall Street Journal reported that both the SEC and the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) had initiated investigations into the events surrounding the acquisition. The following day, in a surprising turn of events, Twitter's general manager Kayvon Beykpour and revenue product lead Bruce Falck were dismissed by Agrawal. Then, on May 13, Musk announced that he had placed the deal "on hold," prompted by revelations that 5 percent of Twitter's daily active users were, in fact, spam accounts, which led to a significant drop of over 10 percent in Twitter's shares. Despite the turbulence, Musk reassured that he was still committed to the acquisition, while Agrawal expressed confidence that the deal would ultimately come to fruition.

As the deal unfolded, Agrawal found himself poised to receive a staggering $39 million from the buyout, while Dorsey would walk away with an impressive $978 million. Behind the scenes, Musk had already chosen a new CEO to take over Agrawal's duties once the acquisition was finalized, though he planned to step in as interim CEO during the transition period that would follow. The next market day brought a harsh reality; Tesla's stock plummeted by over $125 billion, resulting in Musk's net worth taking a $30 billion hit. 

With the acceptance of the deal in the public eye, Musk expressed his ambition to make the tweet-ranking algorithm open-sourced, aiming to foster greater transparency within the platform. He voiced intentions to eliminate spambots and to “authenticate all real humans,” even hinting at the radical idea of transforming Twitter's San Francisco headquarters into a homeless shelter. Musk's faith in Twitter's corporate management wavered, and he confided to banks about his thoughts on slashing executive and board salaries. 

His discontent spilled over into public tweets, where he criticized decisions made by Twitter executives, including Vijaya Gadde, who subsequently faced harassment from users spouting racist and sexist remarks. On April 28, Twitter communicated to advertising agencies that their campaigns would not appear next to any offensive content. In discussions with bankers, Musk floated ideas of cutting jobs and costs, urging influencers to unleash their creativity and considering the addition of subscription services to Twitter.
